Style of garden

The **Chinese garden** is a landscape [garden](/wiki/Garden "Garden") style which has evolved over three thousand years. It includes both the vast gardens of the [Chinese emperors](/wiki/Emperor_of_China "Emperor of China") and members of the imperial family, built for pleasure and to impress, and the more intimate gardens created by scholars, poets, former government officials, soldiers and merchants, made for reflection and escape from the outside world. They create an idealized miniature landscape, which is meant to express the harmony that should exist between [man and nature](/wiki/Man_and_nature "Man and nature").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The art of Chinese garden integrates [architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture"), [calligraphy](/wiki/Chinese_calligraphy "Chinese calligraphy") and [painting](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting"), sculpture, [literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature"), gardening and [other arts](/wiki/Chinese_art "Chinese art"). It is a model of Chinese aesthetics, reflecting the profound philosophical thinking and pursuit of life of the Chinese people. Among them, [Chengde Mountain Resort](/wiki/Chengde_Mountain_Resort "Chengde Mountain Resort")[[2]](#cite_note-2) and the [Summer Palace](/wiki/Summer_Palace "Summer Palace"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) which belong to royal gardens, and several of the [Classical Gardens of Suzhou](/wiki/Classical_Gardens_of_Suzhou "Classical Gardens of Suzhou")[[4]](#cite_note-4) in [Jiangsu Province](/wiki/Jiangsu "Jiangsu"), which belong to private gardens, are also included in the [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). Many essential elements are used in Chinese gardens, and [Moon Gate](/wiki/Moon_gate "Moon gate") is one of them.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

A typical Chinese garden is enclosed by walls and includes one or more ponds, rock works, trees and flowers, and an assortment of halls and pavilions within the garden, connected by winding paths and zig-zag galleries. By moving from structure to structure, visitors can view a series of carefully composed scenes, unrolling like a scroll of landscape paintings.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

The earliest recorded Chinese gardens were created in the valley of the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River"), during the [Shang dynasty](/wiki/Shang_dynasty "Shang dynasty") (1600–1046 BC). These gardens were large enclosed parks where the kings and nobles hunted game, or where fruit and vegetables were grown. Early inscriptions from this period, carved on tortoise shells, have three Chinese characters for garden, *you*, *pu* and *yuan*. *You* was a royal garden where birds and animals were kept, while *pu* was a garden for plants. During the [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") (221–206 BC), *[yuan](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9C%92 "wiktionary:園")* became the character for all gardens.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The old character for *yuan* is a small picture of a garden; it is enclosed in a square which can represent a wall, and has symbols which can represent the plan of a structure, a small square which can represent a pond, and a symbol for a plantation or a pomegranate tree.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

A famous royal garden of the late Shang dynasty was the *Terrace, Pond and Park* of the Spirit (*Lingtai, Lingzhao Lingyou*) built by [King Wenwang](/wiki/King_Wenwang "King Wenwang") west of his capital city, [Yin](/wiki/Yinxu "Yinxu"). The park was described in the *[Classic of Poetry](/wiki/Classic_of_Poetry "Classic of Poetry")* this way:

:   The King makes his promenade in the Park of the Spirit,
:   The deer are kneeling on the grass, feeding their fawns,
:   The deer are beautiful and resplendent.
:   The immaculate cranes have plumes of a brilliant white.
:   The King makes his promenade to the Pond of the Spirit,
:   The water is full of fish, who wriggle.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Another early royal garden was *Shaqui*, or the *Dunes of Sand*, built by the last Shang ruler, [King Zhou](/wiki/King_Zhou_of_Shang "King Zhou of Shang") (1075–1046 BC). It was composed of an earth terrace, or *tai*, which served as an observation platform in the center of a large square park. It was described in one of the early classics of Chinese literature, the *[Records of the Grand Historian](/wiki/Records_of_the_Grand_Historian "Records of the Grand Historian")* (*Shiji*).[[9]](#cite_note-9) According to the *Shiji*, one of the most famous features of this garden was the *Wine Pool and Meat Forest* (酒池肉林). A large pool, big enough for several small boats, was constructed on the palace grounds, with inner linings of polished oval shaped stones from the seashore. The pool was then filled with wine. A small island was constructed in the middle of the pool, where trees were planted, which had skewers of roasted meat hanging from their branches. King Zhou and his friends and concubines drifted in their boats, drinking the wine with their hands and eating the roasted meat from the trees. Later Chinese philosophers and historians cited this garden as an example of decadence and bad taste.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

During the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") (722–481 BC), in 535 BC, the *Terrace of Shanghua*, with lavishly decorated palaces, was built by [King Jing](/wiki/King_Jing_of_Zhou_(Gui) "King Jing of Zhou (Gui)") of the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty"). In 505 BC, an even more elaborate garden, the *Terrace of Gusu*, was begun. It was located on the side of a mountain, and included a series of terraces connected by galleries, along with a lake where boats in the form of blue dragons navigated. From the highest terrace, a view extended as far as [Lake Tai](/wiki/Lake_Tai "Lake Tai"), the Great Lake.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### *The Legend of the Isle of the Immortals*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The Legend of the Isle of the Immortals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/YuanJiang-Penglai_Island.jpg/250px-YuanJiang-Penglai_Island.jpg)](/wiki/File:YuanJiang-Penglai_Island.jpg)

A miniature version of [Mount Penglai](/wiki/Mount_Penglai "Mount Penglai"), the legendary home of the [Eight Immortals](/wiki/Eight_Immortals "Eight Immortals"), was recreated in many classical Chinese gardens

An ancient Chinese legend played an important part in early garden design. In the 4th century BC, a tale in the *[Classic of Mountains and Seas](/wiki/Classic_of_Mountains_and_Seas "Classic of Mountains and Seas")* described a peak called [Mount Penglai](/wiki/Mount_Penglai "Mount Penglai") located on one of three islands at the eastern end of the [Bohai Sea](/wiki/Bohai_Sea "Bohai Sea"), between China and Korea, which was the home of the [Eight Immortals](/wiki/Eight_Immortals "Eight Immortals"). On this island were palaces of gold and silver, with jewels on the trees. There was no pain, no winter, wine glasses and rice bowls were always full, and fruits, when eaten, granted eternal life.

In 221 BC, [Ying Zheng, the King of Qin](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang") conquered other rival states and unified China under the [Qin Empire](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty"), which he ruled until 210 BC. He heard the legend of the islands and sent emissaries to find the islands and bring back the elixir of immortal life, without success. At his palace near his capital, [Xianyang](/wiki/Xianyang "Xianyang"), he created a garden with a large lake called *Lanchi gong* or the *Lake of the Orchids*. On an island in the lake he created a replica of Mount Penglai, symbolizing his search for paradise. After his death, the Qin Empire fell in 206 BC and his capital city and garden were completely destroyed, but the legend continued to inspire Chinese gardens. Some gardens have a single island with an artificial mountain representing the island of the Eight Immortals. Other gardens have gardens featuring three Boshan Mountains - [Penglai](/wiki/Mount_Penglai "Mount Penglai"), Yingzhou, and [Fanghu](/wiki/Fanghu "Fanghu") or Fangzhang. The Yichi Sanshan (Chinese: 一池三山) system of one pond with three mountains has been a main model of royal gardens.
[[12]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._12-12)

### Han dynasty (206 BC–220 AD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Han dynasty (206 BC–220 AD)")]

Under the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (206 BC – 220 AD), a new imperial capital was built at [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an"), and [Emperor Wu](/wiki/Emperor_Wu_of_Han "Emperor Wu of Han") built a new imperial garden, which combined the features of botanical and zoological gardens, as well as the traditional hunting grounds. Inspired by another version of Chinese classic about the Isles of the Immortals, called [Liezi](/wiki/Liezi "Liezi"), he created a large artificial lake, the *Lake of the Supreme Essence*, with three artificial islands in the center representing the three isles of the Immortals - [Penglai](/wiki/Mount_Penglai "Mount Penglai"), [Fanghu](/wiki/Fanghu "Fanghu"), and Yingzhou. The park was later destroyed, but its memory would continue to inspire Chinese garden design for centuries. The Jianzhang Palace in the Han Dynasty is the first known garden built with the complete set of the three remaining Bohai Shenshan mountains. Since then, the Yichi Sanshan (Chinese: 一池三山) system of one pond with three mountains has been a main model of royal gardens.[[12]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._12-12)

Another notable garden of the Han period was the *Garden of General Liang Ji* built under [Emperor Shun](/wiki/Emperor_Shun_of_Han "Emperor Shun of Han") (125–144 AD). Using a fortune amassed during his twenty years in the imperial court, [Liang Ji](/wiki/Liang_Ji "Liang Ji") built an immense landscape garden with artificial mountains, ravines and forests, filled with rare birds and domesticated wild animals. This was one of the first gardens that tried to create an idealized copy of nature.[[12]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._12-12)

### Gardens for poets and scholars (221–618 AD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Gardens for poets and scholars (221–618 AD)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Wang_Xizhi_by_Qian_Xuan.jpg/250px-Wang_Xizhi_by_Qian_Xuan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wang_Xizhi_by_Qian_Xuan.jpg)

The calligrapher [Wang Xizhi](/wiki/Wang_Xizhi "Wang Xizhi") in his garden, the Orchid Pavilion

After the fall of the Han dynasty, a long period of political instability began in China. Buddhism was introduced into China by [Emperor Ming](/wiki/Emperor_Ming_of_Han "Emperor Ming of Han") (57–75 AD), and spread rapidly. By 495, the city of [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang"), capital of the [Northern Wei](/wiki/Northern_Wei "Northern Wei") dynasty, had over 1,300 temples, mostly in the former residences of believers. Each of the temples had its own small garden.[[13]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._16-13)

During this period, many former government officials left the court and built gardens where they could escape the outside world and concentrate on nature and literature. One example was the *Jingu Yuan*, or *Garden of the Golden Valley*, built in 296 by [Shi Chong](/wiki/Shi_Chong "Shi Chong") (249–300 AD), an aristocrat and former court official, ten kilometers northeast of Luoyang. He invited thirty famous poets to a banquet in his garden, and wrote about the event himself:

> I have a country house at the torrent of the Golden Valley...where there is a spring of pure water, a luxuriant wood, fruit trees, bambo, cypress, and medicinal plants. There are fields, two hundred sheep, chickens, pigs, geese and ducks...There is also a water mill, a fish pond, caves, and everything to beguile the eye and please the heart....With my literary friends, we took walks day and night, feasted, climbed a mountain to view the scenery, and sat by the side of the stream.

This visit to the garden resulted in a famous collection of poems, *Jingu Shi*, or *Poems of the Golden Valley*, and launched a long tradition of writing poetry in and about gardens.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The poet and calligrapher [Wang Xizhi](/wiki/Wang_Xizhi "Wang Xizhi") (307–365) wrote in his excellent calligraphy the *[Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion](/wiki/Lantingji_Xu "Lantingji Xu")* introducing a book recording the event of the [Orchid Pavilion Gathering](/wiki/Orchid_Pavilion_Gathering "Orchid Pavilion Gathering"), another famous poetry setting at a country retreat called the "Orchid Pavilion". This was a park with a meandering stream. He brought together a group of famous poets, and seated them beside the stream. Then he placed cups of wine in the stream, and let them float. If the cup stopped beside one of the poets, he was obliged to drink it and then compose a poem. The garden of the floating cup (*liubei tang*), with small pavilions and artificial winding streams, became extremely popular in both imperial and private gardens.[[13]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._16-13)

The Orchid Pavilion inspired [Emperor Yang](/wiki/Emperor_Yang_of_Sui "Emperor Yang of Sui") (604–617) of the [Sui dynasty](/wiki/Sui_dynasty "Sui dynasty") to build his new imperial garden, the *Garden of the West*, near [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"). His garden had a meandering stream for floating glasses of wine and pavilions for writing poetry. He also used the park for theatrical events; he launched small boats on his stream with animated figures illustrating the history of China.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Tang dynasty (618–907), First Golden Age of the Classical Garden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tang dynasty (618–907), First Golden Age of the Classical Garden")]

The [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") (618–907 AD) was considered the first golden age of the classical Chinese garden. [Emperor Xuanzong](/wiki/Emperor_Xuanzong_of_Tang "Emperor Xuanzong of Tang") built a magnificent imperial garden, the *Garden of the Majestic Clear Lake,* near [Xi′an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), and lived there with his famous concubine, [Consort Yang](/wiki/Yang_Guifei "Yang Guifei").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Painting and poetry reached a level never seen before, and new gardens, large and small, filled the capital city, [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an"). The new gardens, were inspired by classical legends and poems. There were *shanchi yuan*, gardens with artificial mountains and ponds, inspired by the legend of the isles of immortals, and *shanting yuan*, gardens with replicas of mountains and small viewing houses, or pavilions. Even ordinary residences had tiny gardens in their courtyards, with terracotta mountains and small ponds.[[13]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._16-13)

These Chinese classical gardens, or scholar's gardens (*wenren yuan*), were inspired by, and in turn inspired, classical Chinese poetry and painting. A notable example was the *Jante Valley Garden* of the poet-painter and civil servant [Wang Wei](/wiki/Wang_Wei_(Tang_dynasty) "Wang Wei (Tang dynasty)") (701–761). He bought the ruined villa of a poet, located near the mouth of a river and a lake. He created twenty small landscape scenes within his garden, with names such as the Garden of Magnolias, the Waving Willows, the Kiosk in the Heart of the Bamboos, the Spring of the Golden Powder, and the View-House beside the Lake. He wrote a poem for each scene in the garden and commissioned a famous artist, to paint scenes of the garden on the walls of his villa. After retiring from the government, he passed his time taking boat trips on the lake, playing the cithare and writing and reciting poetry.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

During the Tang dynasty, plant cultivation was developed to an advanced level, with many plant species being grown by means of plant [introduction](/wiki/Introduced_species "Introduced species"), [domestication](/wiki/Domestication "Domestication"), [transplantation](/wiki/Transplanting "Transplanting"), and [grafting](/wiki/Grafting "Grafting").[[18]](#cite_note-chen-plant-120-18) The aesthetic properties of plants were highlighted, while numerous books on plant classification and cultivation were published.[[18]](#cite_note-chen-plant-120-18) The capital, [Chang'an](/wiki/Chang%27an "Chang'an"), was a very cosmopolitan city, filled with diplomats, merchants, pilgrims, monks and students, who carried descriptions of the gardens all over Asia. The economic prosperity of the Tang dynasty led to the increasing construction of classical gardens across all of China.

The last great garden of the Tang dynasty was the *Hamlet of the Mountain of the Serene Spring* (*Pingquan Shanzhuang*), built east of the city of Luoyang by [Li Deyu](/wiki/Li_Deyu "Li Deyu"), Grand Minister of the Tang Empire. The garden was vast, with over a hundred pavilions and structures, but it was most famous for its collection of exotically shaped rocks and plants, which its creator collected all over China. Rocks of unusual shapes, known as Chinese Scholars' Rocks, often selected to portray the part of a mountain or mountain range in a garden scene, gradually became an essential feature of the Chinese garden.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Song Dynasty (960–1279)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Song Dynasty (960–1279)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/%E5%AE%8B_%E9%87%91%E6%98%8E%E6%B1%A0%E7%88%AD%E6%A8%99%E5%9C%96.jpg/250px-%E5%AE%8B_%E9%87%91%E6%98%8E%E6%B1%A0%E7%88%AD%E6%A8%99%E5%9C%96.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%AE%8B_%E9%87%91%E6%98%8E%E6%B1%A0%E7%88%AD%E6%A8%99%E5%9C%96.jpg)

The Lake of the Clarity of Gold, an artificial lake and pleasure garden built by [Emperor Huizong of Song](/wiki/Emperor_Huizong_of_Song "Emperor Huizong of Song") at his capital, [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng")

There were two periods of the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), northern and southern, and both were known for the construction of famous gardens. [Emperor Huizong](/wiki/Emperor_Huizong_of_Song "Emperor Huizong of Song") (1082–1135) was an accomplished painter of birds and flowers. A scholar himself, he integrated elements of the scholar garden into his grand imperial garden. His first garden, called *The Basin of the Clarity of Gold*, was an artificial lake surrounded by terraces and pavilions. The public was invited into the garden in the spring for boat races and spectacles on the lake. In 1117 he personally supervised the building of a new garden. He had exotic plants and picturesque rocks brought from around China for his garden, particularly the prized rocks from [Lake Tai](/wiki/Lake_Tai "Lake Tai"). Some of the rocks were so large that, in order to move them by water on the grand canal, he had to destroy all the bridges between Hangzhou and Beijing. In the center of his garden he had constructed an artificial mountain a hundred meters high, with cliffs and ravines, which he named *Genyue*, or "The Mountain of Stability." The garden was finished in 1122. In 1127, Emperor Huizong was forced to flee from the Song capital, [Kaifeng](/wiki/Kaifeng "Kaifeng"), when it came under attack by the armies of the [Jurchen](/wiki/Jurchen_people "Jurchen people")-led [Jin dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(1115%E2%80%931234) "Jin dynasty (1115–1234)"). When he returned (as a captive of the Jurchens), he found his garden completely destroyed, all the pavilions burned and the art works looted. Only the mountain remained.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Canglangting_entry_hall.jpg/250px-Canglangting_entry_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canglangting_entry_hall.jpg)

The [Blue Wave Pavilion](/wiki/Blue_Wave_Pavilion "Blue Wave Pavilion") in [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou") (1044), the oldest extant Song Dynasty Garden

While the imperial gardens were the best known, many smaller but equally picturesque gardens were built in cities such as [Luoyang](/wiki/Luoyang "Luoyang"). *The Garden of the Monastery of the Celestial Rulers* in Luoyang was famous for its peonies; the entire city came when they were in bloom. The *Garden of Multiple Springtimes* was famous for its view of the mountains. The most famous garden in Luoyang was *The Garden of Solitary Joy* (*Dule Yuan*), built by the poet and historian [Sima Guang](/wiki/Sima_Guang "Sima Guang") (1021–1086). His garden had an area of eight *mu*, or about 1.5 hectares. In the center was the Pavilion of Study, his library, with five thousand volumes. To the north was an artificial lake, with a small island, with a picturesque fisherman's hut. To the east was a garden of medicinal herbs, and to the west was an artificial mountain, with a belevedere at the summit to view the surrounding neighborhoods. Any passer-by could visit the garden by paying a small fee.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Master_of_the_Nets_Garden_Suzhou.jpg/250px-Master_of_the_Nets_Garden_Suzhou.jpg)](/wiki/File:Master_of_the_Nets_Garden_Suzhou.jpg)

The [Master of the Nets Garden](/wiki/Master_of_the_Nets_Garden "Master of the Nets Garden") in Suzhou (1141) was a model for later scholar's gardens.

After fall of Kaifeng, the capital of the Song dynasty was moved to Lin'an (present-day [Hangzhou](/wiki/Hangzhou "Hangzhou"), [Zhejiang](/wiki/Zhejiang "Zhejiang")). The city of Lin'an soon had more than fifty gardens built on the shore of the Western Lake. The other city in the province famous for its gardens was [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou"), where many scholars, government officials and merchants built residences with gardens. Some of these gardens still exist today, though most have been greatly altered over the centuries.

The oldest Suzhou garden that can be seen today is the [Blue Wave Pavilion](/wiki/Blue_Wave_Pavilion "Blue Wave Pavilion"), built in 1044 by the Song dynasty poet Su Shunqing. (1008–1048). In the Song dynasty, it consisted of a hilltop viewing pavilion. Other lakeside pavilions were added, including a reverence hall, a recitation hall, and a special pavilion for watching the fish. Over the centuries it was much modified, but still keeps its essential plan.

Another Song dynasty garden still in existence is the [Master of the Nets Garden](/wiki/Master_of_the_Nets_Garden "Master of the Nets Garden") in Suzhou. It was created in 1141 by [Shi Zhengzhi](/w/index.php?title=Shi_Zhengzhi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shi Zhengzhi (page does not exist)"), Deputy Civil Service Minister of the Southern Song government. It had his library, the Hall of Ten Thousand Volumes, and an adjacent garden called the Fisherman's Retreat. It was extensively remodeled between 1736 and 1796, but it remains one of the best example of a Song Dynasty Scholars Garden.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

In the city of [Wuxi](/wiki/Wuxi "Wuxi"), on the edge of [Lake Tai](/wiki/Lake_Tai "Lake Tai") and at the foot of two mountains, there were thirty four gardens recorded by the Song dynasty historian Zhou Mi (1232–1308). The two most famous gardens, the *Garden of the North* (*Beiyuan*) and the *Garden of the South* (*Nanyuan*), both belonged to [Shen Dehe](/w/index.php?title=Shen_Dehe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shen Dehe (page does not exist)"), Grand Minister to [Emperor Gaozong](/wiki/Emperor_Gaozong_of_Song "Emperor Gaozong of Song") (1131–1162). The Garden of the South was a classic mountain-and-lake (shanshui) garden; it had a lake with an Island of Immortality (*Penglai dao*), on which were three great boulders from Taihu. The Garden of the South was a water garden, with five large lakes connected to Lake Tai. A terrace gave visitors a view of the lake and the mountains.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Yuan dynasty (1279–1368)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Yuan dynasty (1279–1368)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/20090905_Suzhou_Lion_Grove_Garden_4502.jpg/250px-20090905_Suzhou_Lion_Grove_Garden_4502.jpg)](/wiki/File:20090905_Suzhou_Lion_Grove_Garden_4502.jpg)

The [Lion Grove Garden](/wiki/Lion_Grove_Garden "Lion Grove Garden") in [Suzhou](/wiki/Suzhou "Suzhou") (1342), known for its fantastic and grotesque rocks

In 1271, [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan") established the [Mongol](/wiki/Mongols "Mongols")-led [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") in China. By 1279, he annihilated the last resistance of the Song dynasty and unified China under Mongol rule. He established a new capital on the site of present-day [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), called [Dadu](/wiki/Khanbaliq "Khanbaliq"), the Great Capital.

The most famous garden of the Yuan dynasty was Kublai Khan's summer palace and garden at [Xanadu](/wiki/Xanadu,_Inner_Mongolia "Xanadu, Inner Mongolia"). The Venetian traveler [Marco Polo](/wiki/Marco_Polo "Marco Polo") is believed to have visited Xanadu in about 1275, and described the garden this way:

> "Round this Palace a wall is built, inclosing a compass of 16 miles, and inside the Park there are fountains and rivers and brooks, and beautiful meadows, with all kinds of wild animals (excluding such as are of ferocious nature), which the Emperor has procured and placed there to supply food for his gerfalcons and hawks, which he keeps there in mew. Of these there are more than 200 gerfalcons alone, without reckoning the other hawks. The Khan himself goes every week to see his birds sitting in mew, and sometimes he rides through the park with a leopard behind him on his horse's croup; and then if he sees any animal that takes his fancy, he slips his leopard at it, and the game when taken is made over to feed the hawks in mew. This he does for diversion."[[24]](#cite_note-24)

This brief description later inspired the poem *[Kubla Khan](/wiki/Kubla_Khan "Kubla Khan")* by the English romantic poet, [Samuel Taylor Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge").

When he established his new capital at Dadu, Kublai Khan enlarged the artificial lakes that had been created a century earlier by the Jurchen-led Jin dynasty, and built up the island of Oinghua, creating a striking contrast between curving banks of the lake and garden and the strict geometry of what later became the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") of Beijing. This contrast is still visible today.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Despite the Mongol invasion, the classical Chinese scholar's garden continued to flourish in other parts of China. An excellent example was the [Lion Grove Garden](/wiki/Lion_Grove_Garden "Lion Grove Garden") in Suzhou. It was built in 1342, and took its name from the collection of fantastic and grotesque assemblies of rocks, taken from Lake Tai. Some of them were said to look like the heads of lions. The [Kangxi](/wiki/Kangxi_Emperor "Kangxi Emperor") and [Qianlong](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor") emperors of the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") each visited the garden several times, and used it as model for their own summer garden, the *Garden of Perfect Splendor*, at the [Chengde Mountain Resort](/wiki/Chengde_Mountain_Resort "Chengde Mountain Resort").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 1368, forces of the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), led by [Zhu Yuanzhang](/wiki/Hongwu_Emperor "Hongwu Emperor"), captured Dadu from the Mongols and overthrew the Yuan dynasty. Zhu Yuanzhang ordered the Yuan palaces in Dadu to be burned down.

### Ming dynasty (1368–1644)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Ming dynasty (1368–1644)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Jichang_Yuan.jpg/250px-Jichang_Yuan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jichang_Yuan.jpg)

[Jichang Garden](/wiki/Jichang_Garden "Jichang Garden") in [Wuxi](/wiki/Wuxi "Wuxi") (1506–1521)

The most famous existing garden from the Ming dynasty is the [Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden") in Suzhou. It was built during the reign of the [Zhengde Emperor](/wiki/Zhengde_Emperor "Zhengde Emperor") (1506–1521) by Wang Xianchen, a minor government administrator who retired from government service and devoted himself to his garden. The garden has been much altered since it was built, but the central part has survived; a large pond full of lotus blossoms, surrounded by structures and pavilions designed as viewpoints of the lake and gardens. The park has an island, the Fragrant Isle, shaped like a boat. It also makes good use of the principle of the "borrowed view," (*jiejing*) carefully framing views of the surrounding mountains and a famous view of a distant pagoda.[[27]](#cite_note-Feng_Chaoxiong_pg._6-27)

Another existing garden from the Ming dynasty is the [Lingering Garden](/wiki/Lingering_Garden "Lingering Garden"), also in Suzhou, built during the reign of the [Wanli Emperor](/wiki/Wanli_Emperor "Wanli Emperor") (1573–1620). During the Qing dynasty, twelve tall limestone rocks were added to the garden, symbolizing mountains. The most famous was a picturesque rock called the Auspicious Cloud-Capped Peak, which became a centerpiece of the garden.[[27]](#cite_note-Feng_Chaoxiong_pg._6-27)

A third renowned Ming era garden in Suzhou is the [Garden of Cultivation](/wiki/Garden_of_Cultivation "Garden of Cultivation"), built during the reign of the [Tianqi Emperor](/wiki/Tianqi_Emperor "Tianqi Emperor") (1621–27) by the grandson of [Wen Zhengming](/wiki/Wen_Zhengming "Wen Zhengming"), a famous Ming painter and calligrapher. The garden is built around a pond, with the Longevity Pavilion on the north side, the Fry Pavilion on the east side, a dramatic rock garden on the south, and the creator's study, the Humble House, to the west.[[27]](#cite_note-Feng_Chaoxiong_pg._6-27)

### Qing dynasty (1644–1912)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Qing dynasty (1644–1912)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Marbleboot.jpg/250px-Marbleboot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marbleboot.jpg)

The [Marble Boat](/wiki/Marble_Boat "Marble Boat") pavilion in the garden of the [Summer Palace](/wiki/Summer_Palace "Summer Palace") in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") (1755). After it was destroyed by [an Anglo-French expedition in 1860](/wiki/Second_Opium_War "Second Opium War"), the [Empress Dowager Cixi](/wiki/Empress_Dowager_Cixi "Empress Dowager Cixi") diverted money from the [Beiyang Fleet](/wiki/Beiyang_Fleet "Beiyang Fleet") to have it rebuilt.

The [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") was the last dynasty of China. The most famous gardens in China during this period were the [Summer Palace](/wiki/Summer_Palace "Summer Palace") and the [Old Summer Palace](/wiki/Old_Summer_Palace "Old Summer Palace") in Beijing. Both gardens became symbols of luxury and refinement, and were widely described by European visitors.

Father Attiret, a French Jesuit who became court painter for the [Qianlong Emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor") from 1738 to 1768, described the Jade Terrace of the Isle of Immortality in the Lake of the Summer Palace:

> "That which is a true jewel is a rock or island...which is in the middle of this lake, on which is built a small palace, which contains one hundred rooms or salons...of a beauty and a taste which I am not able to express to you. The view is admirable...[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Their construction and improvement consumed a large part of the imperial treasury. [Empress Dowager Cixi](/wiki/Empress_Dowager_Cixi "Empress Dowager Cixi") famously diverted money intended for the modernization of the [Beiyang Fleet](/wiki/Beiyang_Fleet "Beiyang Fleet") and used it to restore the Summer Palace and the marble teahouse in the shape of boat on [Lake Kunming](/wiki/Lake_Kunming "Lake Kunming"). Both the Summer Palace and Old Summer Palace were destroyed during the [Boxer Rebellion](/wiki/Boxer_Rebellion "Boxer Rebellion") and by punitive expeditions of European armies during the nineteenth century, but are now gradually being restored.

In addition to the Old Summer Palace and Summer Palace, between 1703 and 1792 the Qing emperors built a new complex of gardens and palaces in the mountains 200 kilometers northeast of Beijing, to escape the summer heat of the capital. It was called the [Chengde Mountain Resort](/wiki/Chengde_Mountain_Resort "Chengde Mountain Resort"), and it occupied 560 hectares, with seventy-two separate landscape views, recreating landscapes in miniature from many different parts of China.[[29]](#cite_note-29) This enormous garden has survived relatively intact.

Renowned scholar gardens which still exist from this period include the [Couple's Retreat Garden](/wiki/Couple%27s_Retreat_Garden "Couple's Retreat Garden") (1723–1736) and the [Retreat & Reflection Garden](/wiki/Retreat_%26_Reflection_Garden "Retreat & Reflection Garden") (1885), both in Suzhou.

## Design of the classical garden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Design of the classical garden")]

A Chinese garden was not meant to be seen all at once; the plan of a classical Chinese garden[[30]](#cite_note-30) presented the visitor with a series of perfectly composed and framed glimpses of scenery; a view of a pond, or of a rock, or a grove of bamboo, a blossoming tree, or a view of a distant mountain peak or a pagoda. The 16th-century Chinese writer and philosopher [Ji Cheng](/wiki/Ji_Cheng_(Ming_dynasty) "Ji Cheng (Ming dynasty)") instructed garden builders to "hide the vulgar and the common as far as the eye can see, and include the excellent and the splendid."[[31]](#cite_note-31)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/2004_0927-Suzhou_MasterOfNetGarden_PaintedMap.jpg/250px-2004_0927-Suzhou_MasterOfNetGarden_PaintedMap.jpg)](/wiki/File:2004_0927-Suzhou_MasterOfNetGarden_PaintedMap.jpg)

Painted map of the Master of the Nets Garden begun in 1140, renovated 1736–1796

Some early Western visitors to the imperial Chinese gardens felt they were chaotic, crowded with buildings in different styles, without any seeming order.[[32]](#cite_note-32) But the Jesuit priest [Jean Denis Attiret](/wiki/Jean_Denis_Attiret "Jean Denis Attiret"), who lived in China from 1739 and was a court painter for the [Qianlong Emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor"), observed there was a "beautiful disorder, an anti-symmetry" in the Chinese garden. "One admires the art with which this irregularity is carried out. Everything is in good taste, and so well arranged, that there is not a single view from which all the beauty can be seen; you have to see it piece by piece."[[33]](#cite_note-33) This spatial irregularity often contrasts with the rigid axial layout of the adjoining residence. In Suzhou's Couple's Garden, the main hall, study, and reception rooms are aligned symmetrically along a north–south axis, while the adjacent garden breaks this order with winding paths, angled pavilions, and rockeries placed to obscure direct sight-lines, creating a sense of natural spontaneity and layered views.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Chinese classical gardens varied greatly in size. The largest garden in Suzhou, the [Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden"), was a little over ten hectares in area, with one fifth of the garden occupied by the pond.[[35]](#cite_note-35) But they did not have to be large. [Ji Cheng](/wiki/Ji_Cheng_(Ming_dynasty) "Ji Cheng (Ming dynasty)") built a garden for Wu Youyu, the Treasurer of [Jinling](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), that was just under one hectare in size, and the tour of the garden was only four hundred steps long from the entrance to the last viewing point, but Wu Youyu said it contained all the marvels of the province in a single place.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The classical garden was surrounded by a wall, usually painted white, which served as a pure backdrop for the flowers and trees. A pond of water was usually located in the center. Many structures, large and small, were arranged around the pond. In the garden described by Ji Cheng above, the structures occupied two-thirds of the hectare, while the garden itself occupied the other third. In a scholar garden the central building was usually a library or study, connected by galleries with other pavilions which served as observation points of the garden features. These structures also helped divide the garden into individual scenes or landscapes. The other essential elements of a scholar garden were plants, trees, and rocks, all carefully composed into small perfect landscapes. Scholar gardens also often used what was called "borrowed" scenery (借景 *jiejing*); where unexpected views of scenery outside the garden, such as mountain peaks, seemed to be an extension of the garden itself.[[37]](#cite_note-stepanova162-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Gongwangfu4.jpg/250px-Gongwangfu4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gongwangfu4.jpg)

Pavilion for viewing the rock garden at the [Prince Gong Mansion](/wiki/Prince_Gong_Mansion "Prince Gong Mansion") in Beijing (1777)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/20090905_Suzhou_Couple%27s_Retreat_Garden_4442.jpg/250px-20090905_Suzhou_Couple%27s_Retreat_Garden_4442.jpg)](/wiki/File:20090905_Suzhou_Couple%27s_Retreat_Garden_4442.jpg)

A moon gate from the Couple's Retreat Garden in Suzhou

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Yuyuan_Gardens_-_concrete_bird_design.JPG/250px-Yuyuan_Gardens_-_concrete_bird_design.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yuyuan_Gardens_-_concrete_bird_design.JPG)

Ornamental window frame for garden-viewing in Yuyuan Garden, Shanghai

Chinese gardens are filled with architecture; halls, pavilions, temples, galleries, bridges, kiosks, and towers, occupying a large part of the space. The [Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden") in Suzhou has forty-eight structures, including a residence, several halls for family gatherings and entertainment, eighteen pavilions for viewing different features of the garden, and an assortment of towers, galleries, and bridges, all designed for seeing different parts of the gardens from different points of view.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The garden structures are not designed to dominate the landscape, but to be in harmony with it.

Classical gardens traditionally have these structures:

* The ceremony hall (*ting*), or “room”. A building used for family celebrations or ceremonies, usually with an interior courtyard, not far from the entrance gate.
* The principal pavilion (*da ting*), or “large room”, for the reception of guests, for banquets and for celebrating holidays, such as New Years and the Festival of Lanterns. It often has a veranda around the building to provide cool and shade.
* The pavilion of flowers (*hua ting*), or “flower room”. Located near the residence, this building has a rear courtyard filled with flowers, plants, and a small rock garden.
* The pavilion facing the four directions (*si mian ting*), or “four doors room”. This building has folding or movable walls, for opening up a panoramic view of the garden.
* The lotus pavilion (*he hua ting*), or “lotus room”. Built next to a [lotus](/wiki/Lotus_(plant) "Lotus (plant)") pond, to see the flowers bloom and appreciate their aroma.
* The pavilion of mandarin ducks (*yuan yang ting*), or “mandarin ducks room”. This building is divided into two sections; one facing north used in summer, facing a lotus pond which provided cool air; and the southern part used in winter, with a courtyard planted with pine trees, which remained evergreen, and plum trees, whose blossoms announced the arrival of spring.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In addition to these larger halls and pavilions, the garden is filled with smaller pavilions, (also called *ting*), or “room”, which are designed for providing shelter from the sun or rain, for contemplating a scene, reciting a poem, taking advantage of a breeze, or simply resting. Pavilions might be located where the dawn can best be watched, where the moonlight shines on the water, where autumn foliage is best seen, where the rain can best be heard on the banana leaves, or where the wind whistles through the bamboo stalks. They are sometimes attached to the wall of another building or sometimes stood by themselves at view points of the garden, by a pond or at the top of a hill. They often are open on three sides.

The names of the pavilions in Chinese gardens express the view or experience they offer the visitor:

* The Peak-Worshipping Pavilion (The [Lingering Garden](/wiki/Lingering_Garden "Lingering Garden")) in Suzhou China
* The Hall of Distant Fragrances ([Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden")) in Suzhou China
* The Mountain View Tower ([Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Pavilion of the Moon and Wind ([Master of the Nets Garden](/wiki/Master_of_the_Nets_Garden "Master of the Nets Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Pavilion in the Lotus Breeze ([Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Listening to the Rain Pavilion ([Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Watching the Pines and Appreciating Paintings Hall ([Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Spot of Return for Reading ([Lingering Garden](/wiki/Lingering_Garden "Lingering Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Between the Mountains and the Water Pavilion (The [Couple's Retreat Garden](/wiki/Couple%27s_Retreat_Garden "Couple's Retreat Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Pavilion Leaning on the Jade ([Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Soft Rain Brings Coolness Terrace ([Retreat & Reflection Garden](/wiki/Retreat_%26_Reflection_Garden "Retreat & Reflection Garden")) in Suzhou China
* Lasting Spring and Moon Viewing Tower ([Retreat & Reflection Garden](/wiki/Retreat_%26_Reflection_Garden "Retreat & Reflection Garden")) in Suzhou China

Gardens also often feature two-story towers (*lou* or *ge*), usually at the edge of the garden, with a lower story made of stone and a whitewashed upper story, two-thirds the height of the ground floor, which provided a view from above of certain parts of the garden or the distant scenery.

Some gardens have a picturesque stone pavilion in the form of a boat, located in the pond. (called an *xie*, *fang*, or *shifang*). These generally had three parts; a kiosk with winged gables at the front, a more intimate hall in the center, and a two-story structure with a panoramic view of the pond at the rear.

* Courtyards (yuan). Gardens contain small enclosed court courtyards, offering quiet and solitude for meditation, painting, drinking tea, or playing on the [cithare](/wiki/Cithare "Cithare").

Galleries (*lang*) are narrow covered corridors which connect the buildings, protect the visitors from the rain and sun, and also help divide the garden into different sections. These galleries are rarely straight; they zigzag or are serpentine, following the wall of the garden, the edge of the pond, or climbing the hill of the rock garden. They have small windows, sometimes round or in odd geometric shapes, to give glimpses of the garden or scenery to those passing through.

Windows and doors are an important architectural feature of the Chinese garden. Sometimes they are round ([moon windows](/w/index.php?title=Moon_window&action=edit&redlink=1 "Moon window (page does not exist)") or a [moon gate](/wiki/Moon_gate "Moon gate")) or oval, hexagonal or octagonal, or in the shape of a vase or a piece of fruit. Sometimes they have highly ornamental ceramic frames. The window may carefully frame a branch of a pine tree, or a plum tree in blossom, or another intimate garden scene.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

Bridges are another common feature of the Chinese garden. Like the galleries, they are rarely straight, but zigzag (called the [Nine-turn bridges](/wiki/Nine-turn_bridge "Nine-turn bridge")) or arch over the ponds, suggesting the bridges of rural China, and providing view points of the garden. Bridges are often built from rough timber or stone-slab raised pathways. Some gardens have brightly painted or lacquered bridges, which give a lighthearted feeling to the garden.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Gardens also often include small, austere houses for solitude and meditation, sometimes in the form of rustic fishing huts, and isolated buildings which serve as libraries or studios (*shufang*).[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Artificial mountains and rock gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Artificial mountains and rock gardens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Beijing_Botanical_Garden_-_Oct_09_-_IMG_1161.jpg/250px-Beijing_Botanical_Garden_-_Oct_09_-_IMG_1161.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beijing_Botanical_Garden_-_Oct_09_-_IMG_1161.jpg)

A [scholar rock](/wiki/Scholar_rock "Scholar rock") from [Lake Tai](/wiki/Lake_Tai "Lake Tai") in the [Beijing Botanical Garden](/wiki/Beijing_Botanical_Garden "Beijing Botanical Garden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Gongwangfu7.jpg/250px-Gongwangfu7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gongwangfu7.jpg)

Rock garden at the [Prince Gong Mansion](/wiki/Prince_Gong_Mansion "Prince Gong Mansion") in Beijing, complete with a grotto

The artificial mountain (*jiashan*) or rock garden is an integral element of Chinese classical gardens. The mountain peak was a symbol of virtue, stability and endurance in [Confucian philosophy](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") and in the *[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")*.[[44]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._114-44) A mountain peak on an island was also a central part of the legend of the Isles of the Immortals, and thus became a central element in many classical gardens.

The first rock garden appeared in Chinese garden history in Tu Yuan (literally "Rabbit Garden"), built during the [Western Han dynasty](/wiki/Western_Han_dynasty "Western Han dynasty") (206 BCE – 9 CE).[[45]](#cite_note-45) During the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), the rock was elevated to the status of an art object, judged by its form (*xing*), substance (*zhi*), color (*se*), and texture (*wen*), as well as by its softness, transparency, and other factors. The poet [Bo Juyi](/wiki/Bo_Juyi "Bo Juyi") (772–846) wrote a catalog of the famous rocks of Lake Tai, called *Taihu Shiji*. These rocks, of limestone sculpted by erosion, became the most highly prized for gardens.

During the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), the artificial mountains were made mostly of earth.[[44]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._114-44) But [Emperor Huizong](/wiki/Emperor_Huizong_of_Song_China "Emperor Huizong of Song China") (1100–1125) nearly ruined the economy of the Song Empire by destroying the bridges of the [Grand Canal](/wiki/Grand_Canal_(China) "Grand Canal (China)") so he could carry huge rocks by barge to his imperial garden.[[44]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._114-44) During the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), the use of piles of rocks to create artificial mountains and grottos reached its peak. During the [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty"), the Ming rock gardens were considered too artificial and the new mountains were composed of both rocks and earth.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The artificial mountain in Chinese gardens today usually has a small view pavilion at the summit. In smaller classical gardens, a single [scholar rock](/wiki/Scholar_rock "Scholar rock") represents a mountain, or a row of rocks represents a mountain range.

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Water")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden1.jpg/250px-Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden1.jpg)

A pond or lake is the central element of a Chinese garden. Here is the pond of the Humble Administrator's Garden.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Gongwangfu8.jpg/250px-Gongwangfu8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gongwangfu8.jpg)

Pond at the Prince Gong Mansion, Beijing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Zhanyuan_south_area_water_1.jpg/250px-Zhanyuan_south_area_water_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhanyuan_south_area_water_1.jpg)

Water view at [Zhan Yuan](/wiki/Zhan_Yuan "Zhan Yuan"), Zhongshan

A pond or lake is the central element of a Chinese garden. The main buildings are usually placed beside it, and pavilions surround the lake to see it from different points of view. The garden usually has a pond for [lotus](/wiki/Lotus_(plant) "Lotus (plant)") flowers, with a special pavilion for viewing them. There are usually [goldfish](/wiki/Goldfish "Goldfish") in the pond, with pavilions over the water for viewing them.

The lake or pond has an important symbolic role in the garden. In the *[I Ching](/wiki/I_Ching "I Ching")*, water represents lightness and communication, and carried the food of life on its journey through the valleys and plains. It also is the complement to the mountain, the other central element of the garden, and represents dreams and the infinity of spaces. The shape of the garden pond often hides the edges of the pond from viewers on the other side, giving the illusion that the pond goes on to infinity. The softness of the water contrasts with the solidity of the rocks. The water reflects the sky, and therefore is constantly changing, but even a gentle wind can soften or erase the reflections.[[47]](#cite_note-chen-plant-145-47)[[48]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._118-48)

The lakes and waterside pavilions in Chinese gardens were also influenced by another classic of Chinese literature, the *[Shishuo Xinyu](/wiki/Shishuo_Xinyu "Shishuo Xinyu")* by [Liu Yiqing](/w/index.php?title=Liu_Yiqing&action=edit&redlink=1 "Liu Yiqing (page does not exist)") (403–444), who described the promenades of the [Emperor Jianwen of Jin](/wiki/Emperor_Jianwen_of_Jin "Emperor Jianwen of Jin") along the banks of the Hao and the Pu River, in the Garden of the Splendid Forest (Hualin yuan). Many gardens, particularly in the gardens of [Jiangnan](/wiki/Jiangnan "Jiangnan") and the imperial gardens of northern China, have features and names taken from this work.[[48]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._118-48)

Small gardens have a single lake, with a rock garden, plants and structures around its edge. Middle-sized gardens will have a single lake with one or more streams coming into the lake, with bridges crossing the streams, or a single long lake divided into two bodies of water by a narrow channel crossed by a bridge. In a very large garden like the [Humble Administrator's Garden](/wiki/Humble_Administrator%27s_Garden "Humble Administrator's Garden"), the principal feature of the garden is the large lake with its symbolic islands, symbolizing the isles of the immortals. Streams come into the lake, forming additional scenes. Numerous structures give different views of the water, including a stone boat, a covered bridge, and several pavilions by the side of or over the water.

The streams in the Chinese garden always follow a winding course, and are hidden from time to time by rocks or vegetation. A French Jesuit missionary, Father Attiret, who was a painter in the service of the [Qianlong Emperor](/wiki/Qianlong_Emperor "Qianlong Emperor") from 1738 to 1768, described one garden he saw:

> "The canals are not like those in our country bordered with finely cut stone, but very rustic and lined with pieces or rock, some coming forward, some retreating. which are placed so artistically that you would think it was a work of nature."[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Flowers and trees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chinese_garden&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Flowers and trees")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Gongwangfu1.jpg/250px-Gongwangfu1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gongwangfu1.jpg)

Blossoming tree by the lake at the Prince Gong Mansion in Beijing

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Liuyuan.jpg/250px-Liuyuan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liuyuan.jpg)

In the Lingering Garden in Suzhou, flowers provide a contrast with a scholar stone chosen to represent [Mount Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai_Mountain "Tiantai Mountain"), one of the founding centers of Chinese Buddhism.

Flowers and trees, along with water, rocks and architecture, are the fourth essential element of the Chinese garden. They represent nature in its most vivid form, and contrast with the straight lines of the architecture and the permanence, sharp edges and immobility of the rocks. They change continually with the seasons, and provide both sounds (the sound of rain on banana leaves or the wind in the bamboo) and aromas to please the visitor.

Each flower and tree in the garden had its own symbolic meaning. The pine, [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") and Chinese [plum](/wiki/Plum "Plum") ([Prunus mume](/wiki/Prunus_mume "Prunus mume")) were considered the "[Three Friends of Winter](/wiki/Three_Friends_of_Winter "Three Friends of Winter")" (歲寒三友) by the scholars who created classical gardens, prized for remaining green or blooming in winter. They were often painted together by artists like [Zhao Mengjian](/wiki/Zhao_Mengjian "Zhao Mengjian") (1199–1264). For scholars, the pine was the emblem of longevity and tenacity, as well as constance in friendship. The bamboo, a hollow straw, represented a wise man, modest and seeking knowledge, and was also noted for being flexible in a storm without breaking. Plum trees were revered as the symbol of rebirth after the winter and the arrival of spring. During the [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty"), the favorite tree was the winter plum tree, appreciated for its early pink and white blossoms and sweet aroma.[[50]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._137-50)

The peach tree in the Chinese garden symbolized longevity and immortality. Peaches were associated with the classic story *The Orchard of [Xi Wangmu](/wiki/Xi_Wangmu "Xi Wangmu"), the Queen Mother of the West*. This story said that in Xi Wangmu's legendary orchard, peach trees flowered only after three thousand years, did not produce fruit for another three thousand years, and did not ripen for another three thousand years. Those who ate these peaches became immortal. This legendary orchard was pictured in many Chinese paintings, and inspired many garden scenes.[[51]](#cite_note-51) [Pear](/wiki/Pear "Pear") trees were the symbol of justice and wisdom. The word 'pear' was also a homophone for 'quit' or separate,' and it was considered bad luck to cut a pear, for it would lead to the breakup of a friendship or romance. The pear tree could also symbolize a long friendship or romance, since the tree lived a long time.

The apricot tree symbolized the way of the [mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_(bureaucrat) "Mandarin (bureaucrat)"), or the government official. During the Tang dynasty, those who passed the [imperial examination](/wiki/Imperial_examination "Imperial examination") were rewarded with the banquet in the garden of the apricot trees, or *Xingyuan*.

The fruit of the pomegranate tree was offered to young couples so they would have male children and numerous descendants. The willow tree represented the friendship and the pleasures of life. Guests were offered willow branches as a symbol of friendship.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Of the flowers in the Chinese garden, the most appreciated were the [orchid](/wiki/Orchid "Orchid"), [peony](/wiki/Peony "Peony"), and lotus (*[Nelumbo nucifera](/wiki/Nelumbo_nucifera "Nelumbo nucifera")*). During the [Tang dynasty](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty"), the peony, the symbol of opulence and a flower with a delicate fragrance, was the most celebrated flower in the garden. The poet [Zhou Dunyi](/wiki/Zhou_Dunyi "Zhou Dunyi") wrote a famous elegy to the lotus, comparing it to a *junzi*, a man who possessed integrity and balance. The orchid was the symbol of nobility, and of impossible love, as in the Chinese expression "a faraway orchid in a lonely valley." The lotus was admired for its purity, and its efforts to reach out of the water to flower in the air made it a symbol of the search for knowledge. The [chrysanthemum](/wiki/Chrysanthemum "Chrysanthemum") was elegized the poet [Tao Yuanming](/wiki/Tao_Yuanming "Tao Yuanming"), who surrounded his hermit's hut with the flower, and wrote a famous verse:

> "At the feet of the Eastern fence, I pick a chrysanthemum,
> In the distance, detached and serene, I see the Mountains of the South."[[50]](#cite_note-Che_Bing_Chiu_pg._137-50)

The creators of the Chinese garden were careful to preserve the natural appearance of the landscape. Trimming and root pruning, if done at all, tried to preserve the natural form. Dwarf trees that were gnarled and ancient-looking were particularly prized in the miniature landscapes of Chinese gardens.[[53]](#cite_note-chen-plant-185-53)
